did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper used an ensemble of 7 state-of-the-art Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs) and an Artificial Neural Network (ANN) to classify melanocytic lesions (nevi vs. melanoma) using dermoscopic images from the ISIC 2019 dataset. The study also fused handcrafted asymmetry features with the CNN ensemble for melanoma detection.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The document is explicitly labeled as an 'ORIGINAL ARTICLE' and presents new experimental results. It describes the design of a novel algorithm combining handcrafted asymmetry features with an ensemble of Convolutional Neural Networks, details the methodology (including dataset preprocessing, model architecture, and training parameters), and reports quantitative performance metrics (AUC, sensitivity, specificity) on the ISIC 2019 dataset.

title: Fusion between an Algorithm Based on the Characterization of Melanocytic Lesions’ Asymmetry with an Ensemble of Convolutional Neural Networks for Melanoma Detection
brief_summary: This study addresses the challenge of melanoma detection by fusing handcrafted features representing color asymmetry with an ensemble of seven state-of-the-art Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs). The researchers utilized a subset of the International Skin Imaging Collaboration 2019 dataset, comprising 6,296 nevi and 1,361 melanomas, to train and evaluate their models. The handcrafted asymmetry features were processed by an Artificial Neural Network (ANN), while the CNN ensemble consisted of EfficientNet models. The main evaluation approach involved comparing the performance of the ANN, the CNN ensemble, and their fused predictions using metrics such as Area Under the Curve (AUC), sensitivity, and specificity. The fusion of both approaches achieved the highest performance with an AUC of 0.942, sensitivity of 92%, and specificity of 82%, outperforming the individual ANN (AUC 0.873) and CNN ensemble (AUC 0.938). The results demonstrate that merging dermatologist-derived asymmetry insights with deep learning models enhances melanoma detection accuracy.

lesion_types_studied.details: Primary target class for detection. The study used 1,361 melanoma images from the ISIC 2019 dataset (after filtering for artifacts, 1,301 remained). It is classified as malignant.

Comparator class for binary classification. The study used 6,296 nevus images from the ISIC 2019 dataset (after filtering, 6,371 remained). Includes 'highly atypical nevi' which were noted as false positives for the CNN ensemble.

all_task_types.target: Binary classification of melanocytic lesions into 'melanoma' (malignant) or 'nevus' (benign) classes.
all_task_types.details: The paper explicitly states the task is 'melanoma versus nevus classification'. The models (ANN, CNN ensemble, and fusion) predict discrete diagnostic categories. Performance is evaluated using sensitivity, specificity, balanced accuracy, and AUC. The ANN classifies based on handcrafted asymmetry features, while the CNN ensemble uses EfficientNet models. The fusion combines predictions via average or voting.

datasets_used.name: ISIC 2019
datasets_used.description: A subset of the International Skin Imaging Collaboration 2019 dataset containing dermoscopic images of melanocytic lesions. The authors filtered the original dataset to exclude images with artifacts (e.g., excessive hair, rulers, bubbles) to facilitate handcrafted feature extraction. The subset is publicly available via a Google Drive link provided in the paper.

use.training: Used for training the Artificial Neural Network (ANN) based on asymmetry features and the EfficientNet CNN ensemble. Class imbalance was handled by weighting the melanoma class 4.59 times higher in the loss function.
use.testing: Used for evaluating the performance of the ANN, CNN ensemble, and the fused model using metrics such as AUC, sensitivity, and specificity.
use.other: Used for training the U-Net segmentation model (along with ISIC 2016 and 2018 datasets) to segment lesions from the background.

datasets_used.name: ISIC 2016
datasets_used.description: The International Skin Imaging Collaboration 2016 dataset, used as part of the training data for the U-Net segmentation model.

use.training: Used to train the U-Net model for lesion segmentation.

datasets_used.name: ISIC 2018
datasets_used.description: The International Skin Imaging Collaboration 2018 dataset, used as part of the training data for the U-Net segmentation model.

use.training: Used to train the U-Net model for lesion segmentation.

statistical_methods_used.purpose: To combine the outputs of the Artificial Neural Network (ANN) and the Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) ensemble for melanoma detection.
To combine the predictions of the CNN ensemble models by selecting the most predicted class.
To handle class imbalance during the training of the ANN, weighting the melanoma class 4.59 times higher than the nevus class.
To evaluate and compare the performance of the ANN, CNN ensemble, and fused models.
To evaluate the diagnostic accuracy of the models.
To evaluate the performance of the models, particularly in the context of class imbalance.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Performance comparisons between the ANN, CNN ensemble, and fused models were based on descriptive metrics (AUC, sensitivity, specificity, balanced accuracy) without statistical significance testing.

ISIC 2019 Dataset (filtered subset).notes: The fusion of the ANN (All features) and the CNN Ensemble (mean predictions) achieved the highest performance. The ANN 'All features' model had 11,474 parameters. The CNN ensemble consisted of EfficientNetB0-B7. Class imbalance was handled by weighting the melanoma class 4.59 times higher.

architecture_used.main_model: Two distinct architectures: 1) An Artificial Neural Network (ANN) for handcrafted asymmetry features; 2) An ensemble of 7 Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs) from the EfficientNet family (EfficientNetB0 to EfficientNetB7).
architecture_used.pretraining: The EfficientNet models were initialized with ImageNet weights. The first training phase froze all layers except the final classification layer. The second phase unfroze the weights for fine-tuning.
architecture_used.input_size: For the CNNs: Input images were resized to the specific input dimensions required by each EfficientNet variant (B0-B7). For the ANN: Input vector size of 714 (concatenation of handcrafted asymmetry features across different color spaces).

ANN.input_features: Handcrafted features representing color asymmetry (axial and central symmetry) computed on quadrants and superpixels across RGB, HSV, YCbCr, and CieLab color spaces.

CNN_Ensemble.fusion_method: Average of predictions (mean) and Voting method.

phase_1.frozen_layers: All layers except final classification layer

classification_or_output_head.ANN: Final layer of size 2 with Softmax activation for binary classification (melanoma vs. nevus).
classification_or_output_head.CNN: Original final layer removed and replaced by a softmax layer for binary classification.

preprocessing_techniques: Image segmentation using U-Net to remove perilesional skin and isolate lesions
Filtering of the ISIC 2019 dataset to exclude images with artifacts (e.g., excessive hair, rulers, bubbles)
Transformation of images into superpixel images using the SLIC algorithm (200 superpixels)
Conversion of images into multiple color spaces: RGB, HSV, YCbCr, and CieLab
Normalization of features using the MinMax algorithm
Resizing of input images to specific dimensions required by each EfficientNet variant (B0-B7)
Data augmentation via random rotation, translation, mirror image, and contrast change

data_augmentation_techniques.details: Applied to dermoscopic images during CNN training to improve model generalization.
Applied to dermoscopic images during CNN training to improve model generalization.

Applied to dermoscopic images during CNN training to improve model generalization.

conclusions: Fusing handcrafted expert features representing color asymmetry with an ensemble of seven state-of-the-art Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs) achieved the highest performance for melanoma detection, with an AUC of 0.942, sensitivity of 92%, and specificity of 82%.
The fused approach outperformed the individual Artificial Neural Network (ANN) based on asymmetry features (AUC 0.873) and the CNN ensemble alone (AUC 0.938).

The ANN model based on asymmetry features performed competitively with individual CNNs while utilizing significantly fewer parameters (approximately 11,000) compared to the CNNs (ranging from 5 million to 66 million).
The ANN model provides interpretable predictions based on color asymmetry, which aligns with dermatological diagnostic criteria and may enhance trust and acceptance among medical professionals.

The CNN ensemble tended to misclassify symmetrical lesions with uniform colors as melanomas (false positives), whereas the ANN correctly identified these as nevi; conversely, the ANN missed some symmetrical melanomas that the CNNs detected.
The study acknowledges limitations, including the use of a filtered dataset that excludes artifacts (e.g., hair, rulers), which does not fully reflect real-world clinical scenarios.

The current feature set is specific to asymmetry; the authors suggest that incorporating additional characteristics such as border, size, and texture (e.g., via the ABCD rule) could further improve detection results.
important_remarks: The study uses a filtered subset of the ISIC 2019 dataset (excluding images with artifacts like excessive hair or rulers) to facilitate handcrafted feature extraction, which limits the reflection of real-world clinical scenarios where such artifacts are common.

The model relies exclusively on color asymmetry features; the authors acknowledge that incorporating other ABCD rule characteristics (border, color, diameter) and texture could further improve detection results.

The CNN ensemble exhibited a tendency to misclassify symmetrical lesions with uniform colors as melanomas (false positives), particularly highly atypical nevi, whereas the ANN correctly identified these as nevi.
The ANN model missed some symmetrical melanomas that the CNN ensemble successfully detected, highlighting complementary error profiles between the handcrafted feature approach and deep learning.
No formal statistical hypothesis testing (e.g., p-values) was performed; performance comparisons were based on descriptive metrics (AUC, sensitivity, specificity) without significance testing.
Class imbalance was addressed by weighting the melanoma class 4.59 times higher in the loss function, but the dataset remains imbalanced (6,371 nevi vs. 1,301 melanomas).
The study lacks external validation on independent datasets beyond the ISIC challenge data, limiting generalizability assessment.

The fusion approach improves interpretability by combining dermatologist-derived asymmetry insights with deep learning, potentially enhancing trust among medical professionals compared to black-box CNNs alone.
